signals_found.value: lake wind flattening the clouds over the Loop

opening_excerpt:
The teacher asked if anyone had questions. Your mind went blank and you said nothing, but your heart is still racing.

It's 11pm. You finished your homework an hour ago. Your phone is face-down on the desk. You know you should sleep. But there's a pull — what if someone replied, what if something happened, what if the group chat moved on without you. You flip it over. Nothing urgent. But now you're in it.

The checking becomes the thing. Not the content — the possibility inside the checking. Each notification reinforces the spiral: there might be something, so you look. Nothing, so you look again. The spiral is what the anxiety built.

That's the notification spiral. It will come up throughout this book — not because it's the only thing happening, but because it tends to be underneath most of what we're going to look at.

You're walking to the bus stop on Wacker Drive along the river. The lake wind flattening the clouds over the Loop afternoon hangs humid and pressing all around. Your backpack straps press into both shoulders equally. Tomorrow is the big history test and you studied wrong material. The bus stop bench holds three other kids already waiting. You count—only forty-five minutes until the bus arrives here. Your leg bounces up and down without your control. The tree shade doesn't cool your skin at all now.

Stay with the moment a second longer. The pattern usually shows itself before the explanation does.

Underneath the feeling is a simple mechanism: the notification spiral treats every decision like a permanent threat. When the alarm runs the response, your nervous system treats uncertainty like danger and asks for impossible certainty. That is why ordinary moments feel heavy.

The point is that the notification spiral treats every decision like a permanent threat.
